question: Why does a naive 'SELECT ... WHERE distance(loc, :me) < :r' over a businesses table with separate B-tree indexes on latitude and longitude perform poorly?
options: B-tree indexes cannot store floating-point columns
Latitude and longitude are independent dimensions, so single-column indexes can't efficiently answer a 2D radius query and the engine falls back to a full table scan
The Haversine function is not deterministic, so the query planner refuses to use any index
Relational databases forbid range predicates on more than one column
explanation: A radius query spans two independent axes; an index on lat or long alone can't prune the other dimension, forcing a near-full scan. A geospatial index maps 2D space to a single indexable key.

question: A proximity service for ~200M mostly-static businesses serving ~5,000 searches/sec is best characterized as which kind of workload?
options: Write-heavy with strong-consistency requirements
Read-heavy with mostly-static data, favoring a widely replicated and cached index
Balanced read/write needing two-phase commit on every search
Fan-out-heavy real-time streaming
explanation: Business locations change rarely while searches dominate, so the index can be precomputed, replicated, and aggressively cached with eventual consistency on writes.

question: Two businesses are physically very close but sit on opposite sides of a geohash quadrant boundary, giving them different prefixes. What is the standard fix?
options: Increase the geohash length until both share a prefix
Switch the entire system to a quadtree
Also query the 8 neighboring geohash cells around the center cell, then filter candidates by true geodesic distance
Store each business under a random geohash to spread boundaries out
explanation: Geohash cells fragment near boundaries, so querying the center cell plus its 8 neighbors and re-filtering by Haversine distance captures the close-but-different-prefix points.

question: How does a quadtree differ from a fixed-precision geohash grid for indexing skewed business density?
options: A quadtree uses fixed-size cells everywhere, while geohash adapts to density
A quadtree adaptively subdivides a node into 4 children only when it exceeds a capacity threshold, so dense areas get deeper subdivision than sparse ones
A quadtree stores data on disk while geohash must stay in memory
A quadtree cannot answer k-nearest-neighbor queries
explanation: The quadtree is density-adaptive: dense regions split deeply and sparse regions stay shallow, whereas a geohash uses a uniform precision regardless of how crowded a cell is.

question: Internally, how do Redis GEO commands (GEOADD, GEOSEARCH) implement a geospatial index?
options: They build an in-memory R-tree per key
They store a 52-bit geohash as the score of a sorted set and range-scan that score for radius queries
They keep a separate B-tree per latitude band
They run Dijkstra over a grid graph on each query
explanation: Redis GEO encodes location as a 52-bit geohash used as the sorted-set score, so radius searches become efficient range scans over the sorted set.
